L.label: Comprehensive
L.desc: Full building concept with phased activation plan

XL.label: Transformative
XL.desc: Campus / master plan with phased delivery strategy

55-west-monroe.name: 55 West Monroe
55-west-monroe.unlockHeadline: A ground floor reimagined as the building's front door to the city.
55-west-monroe.unlockSub: The building had the bones. What it lacked was a story. The Urban Eddy gave it one — and the market responded in 18 months.

stats.label: Vacancy Reduction
stats.context: from 41% to 7% within 18 months of project completion

stats.label: Broker Tour Volume
stats.context: increase in scheduled tours in Q1 post-launch

stats.label: Rent Premium
stats.context: above pre-repositioning asking rate on renewed leases

stats.label: Sprint to Signed Leases
stats.context: from concept delivery to first new tenant agreements

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: 41% vacancy in a 40-story Class A asset in the Loop — not a market problem. Leasing teams had no story to tell and brokers weren't bringing tours.

diagnosis.label: The Ground Floor
diagnosis.finding: 2,400 SF of lobby and connector space was being treated as pass-through. No dwell time. No activation. Tenants walked past it twice a day and never thought about it.

diagnosis.label: The Competitive Gap
diagnosis.finding: 12 comparable Loop buildings were fighting on spec and price. None had a distinct narrative. The building that had a story would win.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: The building sits at a natural pedestrian confluence — three CTA lines, two parking structures, a food hall to the east. Design for circulation, not destination. The Urban Eddy was born.

concept.narrative: Four weeks, full design intelligence. We walked the building, mapped the competition, ran a programming workshop with ownership and leasing, developed three distinct concept directions, and delivered a full design language, floor plans, and four photorealistic renderings — plus a broker activation kit ownership could use immediately.
deliverables: Floor plans — lobby, connector, Level 2 terrace

journey.label: Site Visit + Competitive Audit
journey.desc: Walked the building with PM and leasing. Mapped 12 competitive assets within 3 blocks of the Loop. Photographed every dead zone.

journey.label: Programming + Matrix Workshop
journey.desc: Amenity Matrix workshop with ownership. Aligned on 6 high-value interventions from a field of 24. Budget scoped per tier.

journey.label: Concept Design — 3 Directions
journey.desc: Urban Eddy, The Exchange, and Vertical Village developed in parallel. Urban Eddy selected unanimously by ownership team.

journey.label: Delivery Package + Broker Kit
journey.desc: Floor plans, renderings, narrative deck, and broker activation kit delivered. Ownership had something to show the market immediately.

journey.label: Design Development + CDs
journey.desc: Full construction documents. Material specifications. MEP coordination. Contractor bid package issued.

journey.label: Phased Construction
journey.desc: Construction sequenced to maintain 95% tenant operations. Lobby, connector, and Level 2 terrace delivered on schedule.

journey.label: Leasing Activation + Results
journey.desc: Broker launch event in completed lobby. 14 tours in 30 days. First lease signed at +18% premium. Vacancy at 7% within 18 months.

testimonials.quote: The sprint gave us something we didn't have — a story to tell. Within a week of showing the renderings to our top prospects, we had three LOIs on the table. The Urban Eddy concept did the selling before we even opened our mouths.
testimonials.name: Michael R.

testimonials.quote: I've worked with a lot of architects on repositioning studies. NELSON came back in four weeks with a concept that looked like six months of work. The Urban Eddy framing sold itself — brokers were forwarding the deck to clients without us asking.
testimonials.name: Sarah K.

testimonials.quote: The lobby used to feel like a pass-through. Now it's where we hold team lunches and client arrivals. It genuinely changed how our team thinks about coming into the office.
testimonials.name: James T.

testimonials.quote: We were skeptical about a four-week timeline at that price point. The ROI made the case before we could second-guess ourselves. Vacancy went from 41% to 7% in 18 months.
testimonials.name: Patricia L.

241-n-5th.name: 241 N 5TH ST.
241-n-5th.unlockHeadline: A rooftop that became the building's most leasable amenity.
241-n-5th.unlockSub: Three years of underutilization erased in 2.5 weeks. The Social Summit gave the building its highest-value differentiator.

stats.label: Broker Engagement
stats.context: increase in tours following concept release

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: Rooftop floor sat vacant for 3 years. Ownership treated it as storage, not an asset. No leasing team had ever shown it to a prospect.

diagnosis.label: The Missed Asset
diagnosis.finding: The floor had panoramic Minneapolis skyline views on three sides and a 4,200 SF plate — enough for three distinct activation zones with separation.

diagnosis.label: The Market Signal
diagnosis.finding: Every competing Uptown asset was fighting on fitness and bike storage. A rooftop social floor was a category-of-one opportunity.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Program for three distinct tenant types simultaneously — morning coffee culture, midday team meetings, after-hours events. One floor. Three revenue moments.

concept.narrative: Two and a half weeks, one rooftop, three activation zones. The sprint gave ownership the language, the imagery, and the rationale to lead with the rooftop in every leasing conversation.

two22-tower.name: TWO22 TOWER
two22-tower.unlockHeadline: A 2nd floor connector transformed from corridor to destination.
two22-tower.unlockSub: The fastest sprint in the portfolio. Two weeks, one insight, one floor — and a building that had something to say.

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: Leasing team had nothing to show between the lobby and tenant suites. Every tour was a floor plan conversation. They needed a moment.

diagnosis.label: The Hidden Asset
diagnosis.finding: The 2nd floor connector bridged two buildings and a parking structure — 3,800 SF of prime floor plate that no one had ever activated.

diagnosis.label: The Tenant Profile
diagnosis.finding: Heavy financial services and insurance tenancy. The floor needed to feel elevated — not playful. Quiet, high-quality, intentional.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Make the floor feel like a private club, not a coworking add-on. Remove all transactional language. Design for the person who doesn't need to perform productivity.

concept.narrative: Two weeks, one connector floor, and a concept that gave the leasing team their best closing line: 'This floor is yours — no one else in the building has access to it.'

cbre-concourse.name: CBRE CONCOURSE
cbre-concourse.unlockHeadline: A Midtown lobby activation that turned a Class A asset into a talent destination.
cbre-concourse.unlockSub: In Atlanta's most competitive submarket, the building that feels alive wins. The Living Threshold made the case.

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: The lobby looked exactly like it was designed in 2004 — corporate, dated, defensive. It repelled the tech tenants Midtown Atlanta was competing for.

diagnosis.label: The Entry Sequence
diagnosis.finding: Three separate entry points from parking, street, and transit all converged in a 60-foot dead zone. The building was losing its first impression three times over.

diagnosis.label: The Tenant Profile
diagnosis.finding: Target tenants were growth-stage tech companies. They were benchmarking against Ponce City Market and Colony Square. The building wasn't in the conversation.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Introduce a living material system — modular trellis and biophilic layer — that signals innovation without screaming startup. Make the lobby feel like something is alive and growing.

concept.narrative: Two and a half weeks to reframe a building's identity. The Living Threshold introduced a modular biophilic system that could be installed in phases, letting ownership activate the concept without a full construction budget upfront.

1500-spring-garden.name: 1500 SPRING GARDEN
1500-spring-garden.unlockHeadline: A below-grade floor turned into the building's most distinctive amenity.
1500-spring-garden.unlockSub: The floor everyone walked past became the reason tenants signed.

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: Below-grade floor was treated as overflow — bike storage, mechanical, and empty shell. Leasing team apologized for it on every tour.

diagnosis.label: The Hidden Asset
diagnosis.finding: 12-foot ceiling heights, direct courtyard access, and a separate entrance. The floor had the bones for the best amenity in the building.

diagnosis.label: The Market Signal
diagnosis.finding: Philadelphia's Callowhill district was attracting creative and media tenants who valued character over polish. The raw conditions were an asset, not a liability.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Don't renovate it — reveal it. Exposed structure, polished concrete, courtyard activation. The Garden Level became the building's most photographed space.

concept.narrative: Three weeks to transform a liability into a landmark. The Garden Level concept leaned into the building's industrial bones rather than covering them up — and gave the leasing team a space they were proud to show first.

golden-hills.name: GOLDEN HILLS
golden-hills.unlockHeadline: A suburban office park repositioned as a neighborhood destination.
golden-hills.unlockSub: Proof that a targeted sprint can change how an entire submarket perceives an asset.

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: Suburban Class B in a walkable neighborhood corridor — surrounded by coffee shops, restaurants, and retail — but designed to face away from all of it.

diagnosis.label: The Street Presence
diagnosis.finding: The building's best elevation faced a surface parking lot. The activated street was 40 feet away and entirely ignored by the entry sequence.

diagnosis.label: The Tenant Mix
diagnosis.finding: Small creative and professional services firms were leaving for hipper addresses. They wanted the neighborhood, not just the building.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Flip the entry. Reorient the ground floor toward the street. Add a pass-through café program and a landscaped forecourt. Make the building part of the neighborhood, not separate from it.

concept.narrative: Three weeks to give a suburban office building a sense of place. The Neighborhood Anchor concept reoriented the entire ground floor experience toward the street and the surrounding community.

50-south-sixth.name: 50 SOUTH SIXTH
50-south-sixth.unlockHeadline: Minneapolis's skyway system reimagined as a competitive advantage.
50-south-sixth.unlockSub: The building already had the connection. The sprint gave it a reason to exist.

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: The skyway connection was a missed handshake — tenants used it, but the building never captured them. No dwell time, no activation, no brand moment.

diagnosis.label: The Skyway Asset
diagnosis.finding: 50 South Sixth sits at one of the highest-traffic skyway intersections in downtown Minneapolis. The foot traffic was free. The question was what to do with it.

diagnosis.label: The Competitive Gap
diagnosis.finding: Every building in the skyway system used it as a connector. No one had turned it into a destination. The building that did would own the conversation.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Activate the skyway level as a mini marketplace — rotating pop-ups, food program, tenant-facing services. Turn foot traffic into building identity.

concept.narrative: Six weeks to turn a skyway connection into a leasing story. The Skyway Pivot concept gave the building's most-trafficked level a programming strategy that made it feel curated — not just connected.

9320-excelsior.name: 9320 EXCELSIOR
9320-excelsior.unlockHeadline: A four-story suburban building repositioned for the hybrid work era.
9320-excelsior.unlockSub: Small footprint, big idea. The sprint gave the building a post-pandemic identity.

stats.label: Vacancy Reduction
stats.context: from full vacancy to 58% occupied in 12 months

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: Fully vacant 4-story suburban building in a post-pandemic submarket. Previous tenants had consolidated downtown or gone remote. The building needed a new thesis.

diagnosis.label: The Opportunity
diagnosis.finding: 95,000 SF at suburban price points — affordable enough to attract growing small firms who didn't need downtown prestige but wanted downtown-quality amenities.

diagnosis.label: The Design Gap
diagnosis.finding: The interior was beige, dated, and generic. It communicated nothing. Prospective tenants had no reason to choose it over any of four competitors within half a mile.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Position for the hybrid-first team — lean into flexible floor plates, strong natural light, and shared amenities that small firms can't afford alone but love to access.

concept.narrative: Two and a half weeks to give a fully vacant building a reason to exist in the hybrid work era. The Suburban Reset concept gave ownership a clear narrative and a targeted tenant profile to pursue.

crest-ridge.name: CREST RIDGE
crest-ridge.unlockHeadline: A single-tenant campus repositioned for multi-tenant leasing in six months.
crest-ridge.unlockSub: The most complex sprint in the portfolio. One building, one departing anchor, and a full repositioning strategy.

stats.label: Leased at Delivery
stats.context: of total SF committed at building opening

stats.label: Sprint to Delivery
stats.context: repositioning concept to first tenant move-in

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: Anchor tenant vacating 140,000 SF in Minnetonka. Ownership had 6 months to reposition from single-tenant to multi-tenant before carrying costs became critical.

diagnosis.label: The Building
diagnosis.finding: Campus-style floor plates — wide, deep, naturally lit on all four sides. Not ideal for multi-tenant subdivisions without a clear strategy for core and circulation.

diagnosis.label: The Market
diagnosis.finding: Minnetonka suburban market had strong demand for 10,000–30,000 SF suites from healthcare, financial services, and professional services firms. The floor plates could serve all three.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Design a multi-tenant common floor — elevator lobby, conferencing, fitness, café — that each tenant could access as if it belonged to them. Shared infrastructure, private identity.

concept.narrative: Six months, full repositioning. The Multi-Tenant Pivot gave ownership a subdivision strategy, a shared amenity framework, and a leasing narrative that signed three tenants before the building opened.

zna-hq.name: ZNA HQ BUILDING
zna-hq.unlockHeadline: A corporate campus master plan that made the case for staying downtown.
zna-hq.unlockSub: Six weeks to build the retention argument — for a company, its employees, and its real estate.

stats.label: Sprint to Phase 1
stats.context: concept to first phase construction complete

diagnosis.label: The Problem
diagnosis.finding: A Fortune 500 tenant weighing relocation vs. renewal. Their employees wanted hybrid flexibility and amenity-rich environments. The building couldn't make that case on its own.

diagnosis.label: The Campus
diagnosis.finding: 12 floors, single-tenant occupancy, and a below-grade parking structure with a direct connection to the lobby. Three underutilized floors could be transformed without disrupting operations.

diagnosis.label: The Employee Story
diagnosis.finding: The client's internal survey showed employees ranked commute and in-office experience as the two biggest factors in return-to-office decisions. The building had to win on experience.

diagnosis.label: The Unlock Insight
diagnosis.finding: Stop competing with work-from-home. Offer something WFH can't: a campus that feels like a destination, not an obligation. Design for the social and creative work that happens best in person.

concept.narrative: Six weeks, full campus strategy. The Campus Statement sprint gave the client a master plan they could present to employees, a phased activation roadmap they could present to ownership, and a lease renewal argument they could present to their CFO.
deliverables: Common floor programming — fitness, café, collaboration
